figures.caption: Table 1: Merged results across image referring segmentation, image chat, and imagelevel benchmarks. RefCOCO/+/g report cIoU. For MME, we list Perception (P), Cognition (C), and Total (T=P+C). “–” indicates not reported.

Fig. 2: The Perceptio model. The language model takes text queries and image features as input to generate the desired text sequence (B). During training time, segmentation (A) and depth (C) teacher models supervise (via loss functions) the LVLM to accurately generate the intermediate perception tokens and the answer text output tokens.

Fig. 4: Perceptio evaluated on RefCOCO qualitative results. We compare our model with Sa2VA on a referring expression. Instance masks are colorized for visibility; depth maps are shown in grayscale (lighter = nearer). Our predictions align with semantic boundaries better than Sa2VA by capturing the expected depth layering across the image. Note: "×" denotes that no depth perception in Sa2VA.

Fig. 6: (left→ right) Hardblink dataset examples with original images (first column), reconstructed depth maps from our method (second column) and overlay with the original image (third column). The overlayed depth maps show that Perceptio model makes correct decision in alignment with the perceived depth map.

Fig. 3: Comparison between ground-truth and predicted outputs for a scene depicting two players engaged in an ultimate frisbee game. The first row shows the original image, ground-truth segmentation, and depth masks. The second row displays the question and the corresponding model predictions for text, segmentation mask, and depth map, demonstrating accurate recognition of the main objects and spatial depth relationships.

Fig. 7: (left→ right) RefCoCoG dataset samples with the RGB image (first column), ground truth segmentation mask (second column), predicted segmentation mask (third column) and the overlayed predicted mask (fourth column) from the Perceptio model.
